paper.title: And then they were two: detection of non-thermal radio emission from the bow shocks of two runaway stars

extraction.summary: The paper presents radio observations of two runaway stars, BD+43°3654 and BD+60°2522, and their bow shocks. Throughout the text the authors consistently label these objects as runaway stars ejected from their parent clusters, with space velocities of 69–100 km/s and 29–40 km/s respectively. The paper never discusses either star as possibly unbound from the Milky Way, hypervelocity, or escaping the Galaxy. No escape velocities, bound/unbound probabilities, or Galactic-potential orbit integrations are presented. Both objects are therefore excluded from the HVS candidate list.

candidate_groups_considered.description: The two main target stars BD+43°3654 (BSBD43) and BD+60°2522 (BSBD60) and their associated bow shocks, discussed throughout the paper as runaway stars.
candidate_groups_considered.reason: The paper treats both stars as ordinary runaway stars ejected from their birth clusters, with velocities relative to the local ISM of ~69–100 km/s (BSBD43) and ~29–40 km/s (BSBD60). The authors never present them as possibly unbound from the Milky Way or Galactic potential, nor do they calculate escape velocities, bound/unbound probabilities, or orbit integrations. They are therefore excluded per the Stella HVS candidate rules.

source_refs.context: Introduction defines runaway stars as ejected from parent clusters with velocities >30 km/s relative to the ISM, with no mention of Galactic escape.
BD+43°3654 is described as a 'very massive runaway star' with velocity 69–100 km/s; no claim of Galactic-unbound status is made.
BD+60°2522 is described as less massive and moving more slowly; the high peculiar velocity suggests a bow shock, with no claim of unbound status.
Space-velocity subsection calculates peculiar velocities v_tr, v_r, and v_star for both stars, but does not compare them to Galactic escape velocity or assess bound/unbound status.
Conclusions reiterate that the paper reports radio observations of bow shocks from runaway stars, with no mention of Galactic-unbound or hypervelocity status.
